figures.caption: Figure 5. Swept reward distributions over different alpha beta values in Synflownet training

Figure 6. Comparison of scaffold CDFs across different targets (columns) and β values (rows) during SynFlowNet training. In 5/6 of the plots the SynFlownet with annealed α has its reward CDF to the right of that trained via trajectory balance, indicating it is generating a higher proportion of high reward molecules. On the other hand α = 1.2 leads to clear mode collapse, choosing a few high value molecules.
Table 2. Model Configuration and Training Hyperparameters. For all hyperparameters not selected we use prime-rl (Intellect, 2025) defaults.

Figure 1. f -divergences include the α-divergence family which contains both Forward KL (α=0) and Reverse KL / standard Trajectory Balance (α=1) as special cases. Lower α yields a more mode-covering loss, higher α a more mode-seeking one.
Figure 9. Comparison of f -divergence losses with more mode covering on the top and mode seeking on the bottom.
Table 1. Model Training and Optimizer Hyperparameters

Figure 7. Tanimoto diversity of unique samples across varying α and β values for GSK3, sEH, and DRD2 targets during SynFlowNet training.This demonstrates that lower α leads to more diverse molecules and that annealing can also lead to more diverse molecules across a range of settings.
Figure 4. An on policy recreation of the synthetic grid experiment in the main text.
